You are ONE of N parallel CANDIDATE planners (generate-N-then-judge, candidate mode) for goal **G7**. Read the goal item `fetch_item("goals", "G7")` and its linked defects (defects:D36, defects:D37) for the confirmed root cause + suggestedFix. Ground yourself READ-ONLY against the repo (internal/reseq/reseq.go admit SUSPECT branch + Rebaseline, internal/bind/multipath.go rq.Observe :1619-1650 + SetPeerRemote→Rebaseline :2167-2182, internal/device/failover.go concentrator noop, the T38 session-epoch machinery that fixed D12, internal/device/device.go up()).

G7 is DEFECT-SEEDED (skip clarifying). Produce a candidate task-DAG that FIXES defects:D36 (folds in defects:D37): trigger Resequencer.Rebaseline() on a DETECTED PEER RESTART (via the authenticated T38 responder-challenge session-epoch change), wired into BOTH the edge single-concentrator path AND the concentrator per-peer path; AND drive a handshake (re)initiation off the first path StateUp transition (D37, the compounding startup half). Validate with a netns one-sided-restart e2e (both directions: restart-only-edge, restart-only-concentrator; privileged run DEFERRED to the o3 + llm-ubuntu-0 hosts, G2 pattern) asserting reconvergence ~= the ~25s both-ends-fresh baseline, capturing reseq dropSuspect/rebaseline counters + wanbond_session_established 0→1 per direction.

Return your candidate plan as a fenced ```json block ONLY (write NOTHING), shape: { "milestones": [{ "title": "...", "dependsOn": [] }], "tasks": [{ "headline": "...", "description": "...", "acceptance": "...", "suggestedModel": "frontier|standard|fast", "milestone": "<milestone title>", "dependsOn": ["<task headline>"], "ledgerRefs": ["goals:G7", "defects:D36"] }], "rationale": "..." }. Prefer a testable surface→wiring→e2e decomposition; each fix task ledgerRefs the defect(s) it resolves. End with a `### Session summary` block.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Compounding defects:D37 (pre-liveness first init, still open) is tracked separately and may be folded in if cheap. content.text: {"item":{"id":"D36","milestoneId":"M28","status":"root-caused","fields":{"headline":"One-sided restart → multi-minute outage: peer holding the old WG session does not promptly re-handshake","severity":"high","description":"[fixes-doc D2, S1 — production deploy, confirmed repeatedly.] When EITHER end restarts, the peer still holding the old session does not promptly re-handshake; the tunnel stays down for MINUTES until a rekey timer fires. Restart edge → o3 stale → down for minutes; restart o3 → edge stale → down for minutes; only restarting BOTH ~together (both re-initiate from scratch) converges in ~25 s. For a WAN-failover product this is severe — any reboot of the Pi or the concentrator is a multi-minute client outage. NOTE the layer: this is the INNER WG session going stale, distinct from the already-resolved OUTER probe/liveness restart deadlock (defects:D12, fixed by the T38 responder-challenge session epoch) — outer paths recover, but the stale WG keypair on the surviving side is not superseded. Compounded by fixes-doc D3 (see the startup-handshake defect): the restarted side's first init fires before path liveness and is not aggressively retried.","suggestedFix":"Extend the trusted resequencer re-anchor (Rebaseline) from the hub-failover-only trigger to a detected PEER RESTART, on BOTH the edge non-failover path AND the concentrator role. The T38 responder-challenge session-epoch (which already authenticates and detects a peer's new session on the OUTER plane, fixing D12) is the natural trusted control event: when a peer's authenticated session epoch changes (restart), call the peer's Resequencer.Rebaseline() so the next inbound DATA frame (the wrapped WG init/response) re-anchors `next` instead of being SUSPECT-dropped. Wire it in the concentrator per-peer path and the edge single-concentrator path (both currently lack any Rebaseline trigger). VALIDATE with a netns one-sided-restart e2e (deferred to the o3 + llm-ubuntu-0 hosts, G2 pattern): saturate the bond so the surviving resequencer's `next` advances past the window, then restart ONLY the edge (run A) and ONLY the concentrator (run B); assert reconvergence well under the WG rekey timeout (target ~= the ~25s both-ends-fresh baseline) and capture reseq dropSuspect/rebaseline counters + the wanbond_session_established 0→1 timestamp per direction. NOTE: static analysis predicts the edge-restart direction should recover in ~10s absent the drop, so the e2e also quantifies the true per-direction magnitude (field-reported as minutes).","sourceRefs":["wanbond-fixes.md §A D2","wanbond-fixes.md §C C5"],"ledgerRefs":["defects:D12"],"tags":["production-deploy","restart","re-handshake"],"rootCause":"CONFIRMED (H2). The multi-minute one-sided-restart outage is the OUTER-plane per-peer resequencer dropping the restarted peer's low-outer-seq frames as SUSPECT — the SAME mechanism as the hardware-root-caused D32, but on the one-sided-restart path which has no rescue trigger. Every inner WG datagram (including the opaque handshake initiation/response) is wrapped in an outer DATA frame and pushed through the peer's resequencer (internal/bind/multipath.go:1619-1650 rq.Observe). After a peer restarts, its outer-seq resets to ~1, far below the stale-high release point `next` the pre-restart high-rate stream advanced the surviving side's resequencer to; admit() (internal/reseq/reseq.go:285-297) treats any frame >1 window below `next` as SUSPECT and drops it (dropSuspect++) unless the unauthenticated tryResync corroborates 3 distinct low seqs within one window — which a freshly re-handshaking peer emitting ~1 DATA frame per RekeyTimeout cannot achieve in time (reseq.go:529-546, the Rebaseline doc naming exactly this as D32). The Rebaseline() trusted re-anchor that defeats this is wired to EXACTLY ONE caller, SetPeerRemote (multipath.go:2167-2182), whose only callers are the edge hub-failover controller — so a plain one-sided EDGE restart (live paths, no endpoint change) never triggers it, and the CONCENTRATOR role runs no failover at all (failover.go:496 noop), so neither side re-anchors on a plain restart. RULED OUT: a WG-layer supersede failure (H2(a)) — amneziawg-go's responder consumes+responds to a fresh init immediately (receive.go:399-417, gated only by tai64n + a 20ms flood window), so the stall is NOT the engine holding the old keypair; the init simply never reaches the engine because the resequencer drops it. COMPOUNDING: the restarted side's first init fires pre-liveness (D37) and is not re-driven off the first path-up edge, but WG's 5s retransmit bounds that to seconds.","sessionLogs":[".cq/logs/20260714-090009-a8d87a208742a8de8.md"],"rawLogs":[".cq/logs/raw/20260714-090009-a8d87a208742a8de8.jsonl"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-13T22:48:32.358Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T09:00:55.833Z","author":"fable-5","session":"671d5adc-7e2a-440e-b87d-6da40edeb7b7"}}

126	func (w *lossWindow) observe(seq uint64) {

128			w.have = true
129			w.first = seq
130			w.highest = seq
131			for i := range w.recv {
132				w.recv[i] = false
133			}
134			w.recv[w.slot(seq)] = true
135			return
136		}
137		if seq > w.highest {
138			// The positions (highest, seq] are newly exposed by the window advancing;
139			// mark them missing, then record the one that actually arrived.
140			if seq-w.highest >= uint64(w.win) {
141				for i := range w.recv {
142					w.recv[i] = false
143				}
144			} else {
145				for k := w.highest + 1; k <= seq; k++ {
146					w.recv[w.slot(k)] = false
147				}
148			}
149			w.recv[w.slot(seq)] = true
150			w.highest = seq
151			return
152		}
153		// Late/reordered arrival: fill its slot only if it still falls in the window.
154		if w.highest-seq < uint64(w.win) {

162		}

165			lower = w.highest + 1 - uint64(w.win)
166		}
167		if lower < w.first {
168			lower = w.first // never charge loss for sequences before the first observed
169		}
170		n := w.highest - lower + 1
171		missing := 0
172		for k := lower; k <= w.highest; k++ {
173			if !w.recv[w.slot(k)] {

175			}

159	func (w *lossWindow) fraction() float64 {
160		if !w.have {
161			return 0
162		}
163		lower := uint64(0)

165			lower = w.highest + 1 - uint64(w.win)
166		}
167		if lower < w.first {
168			lower = w.first // never charge loss for sequences before the first observed
169		}
170		n := w.highest - lower + 1
171		missing := 0
172		for k := lower; k <= w.highest; k++ {
173			if !w.recv[w.slot(k)] {
174				missing++
175			}
176		}
177		return float64(missing) / float64(n)

183	// (T18), so this is explicitly NOT a per-path metric: feeding it a single path's
184	// frames would read scheduler striping as loss. Feed it EVERY received DATA

91					dtSeconds := float64(nowNanos-prev.atNanos) / 1e9
92					if dtSeconds > 0 && byteTotal >= prev.total {
93						tput = float64(byteTotal-prev.total) * 8 / dtSeconds
94					}
95				}
96				s.last[key] = byteSample{total: byteTotal, atNanos: nowNanos}
97				out = append(out, metrics.PathSnapshot{
98					Peer:                    peer.Name,
99					Name:                    t.Name,
100					TxBytes:                 t.TxBytes,
101					RxBytes:                 t.RxBytes,
102					ThroughputBitsPerSecond: tput,
103					Estimate:                t.Estimate,
104					State:                   t.State,
105				})
106			}
107		}
108		return out
109	}
110	
111	// FEC implements metrics.Source: it reads the Bind's per-peer connection-scoped FEC
112	// counters (T24, T94) verbatim at scrape time, one entry per bound peer. Unlike the
113	// per-path throughput, the FEC counters are cumulative and need no rate derivation or
114	// prior-sample state, so this is a direct pass-through of the Bind's lock-free snapshot.
115	func (s *metricsSource) FEC() []metrics.FECSnapshot {
116		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
117		out := make([]metrics.FECSnapshot, len(peers))
118		for i, peer := range peers {
119			f := peer.FEC
120			out[i] = metrics.FECSnapshot{
121				Peer:                 peer.Name,
122				DataPackets:          f.DataFrames,
123				RepairPackets:        f.ParityFrames,
124				RecoveredPackets:     f.Recovered,
125				UnrecoverablePackets: f.Unrecoverable,
126				DataBytes:            f.DataBytes,
127				RepairBytes:          f.ParityBytes,
128				ResidualLossRatio:    f.ResidualLoss,
129			}
130		}
131		return out
132	}
133	
134	// Reseq implements metrics.Source: it reads the Bind's per-peer resequencer counters
135	// (T94) verbatim at scrape time, one entry per bound peer — a direct pass-through of
136	// the resequencer's own cumulative Stats(), like FEC needing no rate derivation.
137	func (s *metricsSource) Reseq() []metrics.ReseqSnapshot {
138		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
139		out := make([]metrics.ReseqSnapshot, len(peers))
140		for i, peer := range peers {
141			out[i] = metrics.ReseqSnapshot{Peer: peer.Name, Stats: peer.Reseq}
142		}
143		return out
144	}
145	
146	// PeerNames implements metrics.Source: it returns the current bound-peer name set,
147	// queried once by metrics.NewCollector to fix the `peer` label's presence for the
148	// collector's whole life (T94) — see the metrics package doc for the back-compat rule.
149	func (s *metricsSource) PeerNames() []string {
150		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
151		names := make([]string, len(peers))
152		for i, peer := range peers {
153			names[i] = peer.Name
154		}
155		return names
156	}
157	
158	// Session implements metrics.Source: it reads the connection-scoped WG-session snapshot
159	// (I2) from the engine seam at scrape time. Like FEC it is a direct pass-through of a
160	// snapshot the underlying monitor computes on demand — no per-scrape state is kept here
161	// (the 0->1 edge log is driven by the separate session-monitor poll loop, not by scrapes).
162	func (s *metricsSource) Session() metrics.SessionSnapshot {
163		return s.session.SessionSnapshot()
164	}

Grounding is complete. The decisive facts:

- The T38 session epoch is per-BOOT, shared across all of a peer's probers (probe.go:130 "All Probers of one boot share the same sessionID"), so a peer-level restart event can be deduped by sessionID.
- The authenticated restart-detection point already exists: `Reflector.acceptLocked`'s adoption branch (probe.go:384-398, "Cross-session probe echoing our LIVE challenge... Adopt the new epoch") — it just surfaces nothing to its caller.
- The Reflector is per-peer state (`newPeerState` → `NewReflector`, multipath.go:255-265) and `Reflect` is called from the shared `dispatchInbound` probe branch (multipath.go:1689-1695), which also holds the peer's resequencer — so wiring Rebaseline at that seam covers BOTH the edge single-concentrator path AND the concentrator per-peer path in one place, with no device-layer coupling for D36.
- `Rebaseline` (reseq.go:546-557) is idempotent, safe pre-first-Observe, takes its own mutex (must not be nested under m.mu — the SetPeerRemote precedent at multipath.go:2177-2182), and its `rebaselines` counter is already in `Stats` and exposed via metrics.
- For D37, the exact Down→Up observation point is the sticky `everUp` latch at multipath.go:1677-1685, and `deviceRehandshake` (failover.go:426-439) already implements the engine poke including the lastSentHandshake backdating that defeats RekeyTimeout suppression of the wasted pre-liveness init.

### Session summary
- **Did:** CANDIDATE-mode grounding for G7 — fetched G7/D36/D37 and explored reseq.go (admit/tryResync/Rebaseline), multipath.go (dispatchInbound, SetPeerRemote, newPeerState, everUp latch), probe.go (Reflector adoption branch, per-boot sessionID), failover.go (deviceRehandshake, concentrator noop), session.go (established-edge monitor); emitted a 7-task, 3-milestone candidate DAG.
- **Achieved:** Candidate plan proposing: surface the authenticated session-epoch adoption event from Reflector (peer-level, deduped by per-boot sessionID), wire it to per-peer Rebaseline in the shared dispatchInbound seam (covers edge AND concentrator uniformly), drive a forced handshake initiation off the first-path-up everUp edge (D37), then a netns one-sided-restart e2e (both directions, G2 deferred-privileged pattern) plus doc sync. No ledger writes.
- **Discovered:** The single most plan-shaping fact: because the Reflector and resequencer are BOTH per-peer state reached from the shared dispatchInbound path, one bind-level wiring point fixes both roles — no role-specific (failover.go/device.go) plumbing is needed for D36; D37 alone needs a device-layer seam. Also: the wasted boot init may stamp lastSentHandshake, so a naive SendHandshakeInitiation(false) on path-up can be suppressed by the RekeyTimeout guard — the deviceRehandshake backdating pattern is the proven counter.
- **Issues:** none blocking; the e2e's reconvergence threshold needs a concrete number at implementation time (baseline ~25s; assert well under the WG rekey timer) and the privileged run is deferred to the o3 + llm-ubuntu-0 hosts per the G2 pattern.
